title: Indonesia Sumatra: Earthy, Herbal, and Dark Roast Espresso with Thick Crema
hero_image_alt: A double espresso in a heavy ceramic cup with exceptionally thick dark brown crema, wisps of steam rising, a few whole cloves and a stick of cinnamon on a dark wooden board beside the cup, dried tobacco leaves and fresh herbs arranged in the background, warm low-angle light creating a moody atmospheric scene
intro: If you love espresso that is bold, earthy, and unapologetically intense, Sumatran coffee is your origin. Grown in the volcanic highlands of Indonesia's largest island, Sumatra produces coffee with a character unlike anything else in the specialty world: deep earthy tones, herbal and spicy notes, a body so heavy it feels almost chewy, and a dark roast profile that produces some of the thickest crema you will ever see. This is the origin for people who want their espresso to feel substantial — a coffee that fills the mouth and lingers long after the last sip.

body.heading: Sumatra's Unique Processing: Giling Basah
body.content: The flavour of Sumatran coffee cannot be separated from its processing method. While most coffee-producing countries use washed or natural processing, Sumatra employs a semi-washed technique called giling basah — literally 'wet hulling' in Bahasa Indonesia. In this method, farmers pulp the cherry, briefly ferment the mucilage-coated parchment overnight, and then sell the still-wet parchment coffee to collectors. The collectors hull the parchment while the beans are still at thirty to thirty-five percent moisture — far wetter than the ten to twelve percent at which coffee is typically hulled in other countries. The exposed, still-damp green beans are then dried on patios to the target moisture level. This aggressive early hulling has profound effects on the bean and the cup. The physical trauma of hulling at high moisture gives Sumatran beans their characteristic irregular, slightly rough appearance — they look less polished than African or Central American beans. More importantly, the extended exposure to humidity and the shortened drying time create conditions for distinctive biochemical reactions. The result is a flavour profile dominated by earthy, herbal, and woody notes: cedar, tobacco, dark chocolate, mushroom, damp earth, clove, and sometimes a savoury, almost brothy quality. These are flavours that would be considered defects in a washed Ethiopian or Colombian coffee but are considered desirable and defining in Sumatran coffee — they are what makes Sumatra taste like Sumatra. The main growing regions — Aceh (Gayo Highlands), North Sumatra (Lintong and Mandailing), and Lampung — each produce variations on this theme, with Aceh coffees tending toward the cleanest and most complex, Lintong offering more herbal intensity, and Mandailing delivering the heaviest body and earthiest character.

body.heading: Flavour Profile: Earth, Herbs, and Dark Chocolate
body.content: Sumatran espresso is the polar opposite of Ethiopian or Kenyan coffee in almost every dimension. Where those origins lead with bright acidity and fruit, Sumatra leads with body and earthiness. The acidity is almost non-existent — a quality that makes Sumatran coffee profoundly comforting to people who find bright, acidic espresso uncomfortable. The dominant flavour notes are dark chocolate, earth, cedar wood, dried herbs (rosemary, sage), tobacco, and a savoury depth that some describe as umami. Behind these primary notes, you may find clove, black pepper, leather, and occasionally a sweetness reminiscent of dark brown sugar or molasses. The body is the heaviest of any commonly available single origin — thick, creamy, almost syrupy, with a mouthfeel that coats the palate and persists long after swallowing. Crema production from Sumatran beans is exceptional, particularly when dark-roasted. The combination of oils released by darker roasting and the unique moisture history of giling basah processing creates an espresso that produces thick, stable, dark brown crema that can stand for three to four minutes before dissipating. For dark roast enthusiasts, this visual and textural richness is part of Sumatra's appeal — the shot looks and feels as substantial as it tastes. Medium-dark to dark roasts bring out the best in Sumatran espresso, developing the chocolate and spice notes while amplifying the earthy character. Very light roasts of Sumatran coffee tend to taste grassy and vegetal in espresso — the earthy compounds that taste rich and complex at darker roast levels come across as raw and unpleasant when underdeveloped. Specialty roasters are increasingly producing 'clean' Sumatran lots that minimise the most aggressive earthy notes while preserving the body and dark chocolate, and these lots can work at medium roast levels for espresso, offering a more accessible entry point for drinkers exploring this origin for the first time.

body.heading: Grinding and Brewing for Maximum Body
body.content: Sumatran beans behave differently under the grinder than most other origins because of the physical changes caused by giling basah processing. The beans are slightly softer and more porous than typical washed coffees, which means they shatter more easily and produce a higher proportion of fine particles. This increased fines production creates a denser puck that naturally slows water flow, so you will typically need to grind slightly coarser for Sumatran coffee than you would for a Colombian or Ethiopian at the same dose and ratio. On the Arco Macinino or Preciso, start one to two steps coarser than your usual espresso setting. On the Arco Zero, open the adjustment by a quarter-turn and taste. Aim for a 1:2 ratio — eighteen grams in, thirty-six grams out — over twenty-five to thirty seconds. If the shot runs slower than thirty seconds, grind coarser; the high fines content means a too-fine grind will choke the puck almost immediately. A slightly faster extraction — even twenty-three to twenty-four seconds — still produces a satisfying cup with Sumatran coffee because the heavy body and low acidity are preserved across a wide extraction range. For temperature, brew hot: ninety-four to ninety-five degrees Celsius. The absence of bright acids means higher temperatures will not create harshness, and the extra heat helps fully develop the dark chocolate and spice compounds. On the Arco Primo, Sumatran coffee is one of the most forgiving origins to brew because the thermoblock's natural temperature range aligns perfectly with what this coffee needs. On the Arco Doppio or Studio, set the PID to ninety-four point five and leave it. Pre-infusion is useful for Sumatran coffee but keep it brief — two seconds maximum. The higher fines content in the puck absorbs water quickly, and extended pre-infusion can create a saturated puck that chokes under full pressure. A short, punchy pre-infusion followed by a firm nine-bar ramp-up produces the best results: a thick stream of dark espresso with crema so dense it almost looks like chocolate mousse.

body.heading: Blending, Milk Drinks, and Pairing Ideas
body.content: Sumatra's most important role in espresso is as a body component in blends. Italian roasters have used Sumatran (and Indonesian coffees generally) for generations to add weight, earthiness, and crema thickness to their blends. A classic Italian espresso blend might contain fifteen to twenty-five percent Sumatran alongside Brazilian base and African brightness — the Sumatran component provides the heavy body and thick crema that define the traditional Italian espresso experience. If you are experimenting with home blending, Sumatran coffee is the ingredient that makes a blend feel substantial in the mouth. As a single-origin espresso, Sumatra works beautifully in milk drinks where the heavy body and dark chocolate notes can shine. A latte with Sumatran espresso has a rich, almost chocolate-milk quality that requires no added sweetener. Cappuccinos gain a depth and weight that lighter origins cannot provide — the thick crema integrates with foam to create a velvety, satisfying drink. For a unique preparation, try a Sumatran espresso with a splash of coconut milk — the tropical earthiness of the coffee harmonises surprisingly well with coconut's natural sweetness and fat content. For food pairings, Sumatran espresso is the natural companion to dark, rich foods: dark chocolate truffles, walnut brownies, aged cheese, dried fruit and nut mixtures, and anything with earthy or umami flavours. The herbal notes pair well with rosemary focaccia or olive oil cake. For breakfast, Sumatran espresso alongside avocado toast with a pinch of sea salt is an outstanding savoury pairing that plays to the coffee's earthy, herbal character. After dinner, a short Sumatran ristretto alongside a square of seventy-five percent dark chocolate and a small glass of aged port creates one of the most indulgent flavour combinations available to the home barista — earth, chocolate, fruit, and warmth in perfect harmony.

key_takeaways: Sumatra's giling basah (wet hulling) processing creates the distinctive earthy, herbal, heavy-bodied character unlike any other origin.
Expect dark chocolate, cedar, tobacco, herbs, and near-zero acidity — the most comforting single-origin espresso for dark roast lovers.
Grind slightly coarser than usual due to higher fines production, and brew hot at 94-95 C to develop chocolate and spice.
Exceptional crema thickness and body make Sumatra outstanding in milk drinks and as a blending component (15-25% of a blend).
Pair with dark chocolate, aged cheese, walnut brownies, and earthy savoury foods that complement the herbal depth.

cta.label: Start Brewing with the Arco Primo
